speaker: interviewer
text: Interviewing Halina--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nelken.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Nelken. And this is tape 4, November 22-- November 24, 1995. Could you describe, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] essentially, what you ate when you were in the ghetto, and whether or not you ever went hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, as a matter of fact, hungry from about almost few months after-- not hungry, hungry, but not like this-- few months after the war. And the food was mostly potatoes, sometimes frozen. Well, rutabaga-- what-- how do you call it-- or some, very seldom, meat. Mostly potatoes and margarine and some marmalade made out of pumpkin or, or beets. And not good beets, but beets that actually the cattle eat. And so this was mostly the things. And then they made-- that Judenrat made something like a kitchen for, for people. And all the time, there were potatoes with a sauce out of mustard. I don't know why mustard was there but other things not. So actually it was and, and-- there was no tea. There was no-- it was ersatz, ersatz coffee and ersatz tea, and, and sugar almost nonexistent, very small. So this was very, very bad. As far as--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the food? Where did it come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were stores, and we had rations, that rations. Well, the rations were very little, but it was something.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it paid for?

speaker: interviewee
text: With money. Well, after all, we were being-- we were making-- even I, in the Fliegerhorst, well, got some money. And whenever I got that money, then I bought bread and some butter and proudly brought it home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a black market on these kinds of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, heavens, there was black market. You could get everything in this ghetto. But you had to have money. And there were plenty of people. There were some Jews, who were the German Jews, who were being thrown out by Hitler to-- from Germany to Poland, just before the war. And they were these ones-- they were collaborators with the Germans. And they were-- everybody knew about it. Everybody was afraid of them. And they had a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So some of the Jews got rich on this suffering.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Oh, boy, of course. And, and well, when, when we did not have money, then, then my mother was selling or father sold a watch or, or, or earrings, or something, you know, like this. But not everybody was well. A lot of people were hungry. And a lot of people were very much impoverished.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often were you-- did you go hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not even think about it. Because, really, this was that I thought, OK, this is my punishment that I never wanted to eat when I could before. So here, now, I don't have these goodies, well. But still, somehow, we'd exist. But we were living by culture, so to speak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meaning that we had still our books. And we were-- there were concert organized. There were many talented people. And therefore, they were very often in that orphanage. Or there were two or one coffeehouse, where there were concert, where people were singing. When-- I remember one when brother was-- my brother was playing-- aside of working at the hospital-- he was playing piano in this cafe or in this concert. He was playing, accompanying an engineer, who played by Wieniawski and then also that-- the Violin Concerto by Mendelssohn. And he was playing it on violin, and my brother at the piano. And  whenever I hear this, [SINGING] I see this room where they were playing this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, and they were-- or sometimes there was an evening organized of the Jewish songs and other songs. And they  were always well-attended. And, and it was wonderful to have something else, not only this misery of, of, of this thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that when once my uncle brought the German paper I think it was from November. I have in my diary this. And there was an article about Jews are-- it is Jewish fault for everything. Jews are guilty of everything. And then we were reading this loud at home with neighbors. Because there was, of course, a curfew, and since 9 o'clock, everybody in the evening had to be home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So neighbors were coming, and we were playing piano. We were reciting. We were discussing poems or literature, whatever. And then this about Jews, they are so guilty. And everybody was so depressed. And they said it's terrible, it's terrible. There is no way out of this. And I remember I went into my little-- well, oh, boy, what is that English word? In between two-- Hallway?

speaker: interviewee
text: No hallway. In between--

speaker: interviewer
text: Doorway?

speaker: interviewee
text: --two walls, in between two walls here, how do you call it? This little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Corner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Corner, yeah. See? Corner. I went into my little corner in my room, and I wrote a poem. And I wrote a poem about this-- that it is-- it's very easy to complain all the time how we are being harmed, and how everything is terrible, and what to do. And maybe we should run away, maybe we shouldn't. This is easy. But what is not easy is, against all odds, to get up and keep clear mind and hope that something will change and look over the walls into the sun. And you see, now while I was transcribing my diary-- well, into here, in Cambridge, into, on, on my typewriter-- I look at this poem. And I thought, my goodness, for the first time, I noticed here that my inner resistance was born. Then, because I was trying not to give in to the despair, to everything, that it is absolutely important to keep that inner strength.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, we were trying to keep our spirits up, regarding of, of-- regardless of everything else. I knew that there were people who were fighting with-- first of all, my brother was, and my father, were, were also connected, with-- were also connected with, with some, you know, this, this resistance. As long we were not in ghetto, my father was always listening to the radio. And  that other Polish friend was sending, that-- so to speak, information to other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was a Jewish resistance. I knew that it's there. But there was no way of getting. First of all, I was too young. Number two, you cannot just go and join them. You have to be taken by them. And people who were there, and now I know that they were many of our friends, they were not-- they were very careful not to talk because they were afraid of the German and others collaborators. Because there were Jewish collaborators, too, shame to say, but there were. So-- but one whom I know and who was wonderful, really, when I talked to him once, and I thought he looked-- remember that I want also do something, I want to be useful. I want to have some projects or, or, or, you know, plan to do. And I remember when he was smiling and looking at me and said, you already have this. You have a very important mission. You will survive, and you will make all of us immortal. This is what he said. I never saw him again. He perished. I, I-- after the war, I've met his wife. He was young, but for a very short time he was married. But this was so. I sometimes, when I read--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remember-- do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. Jozef--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jozef, J-O-- J-O-Z-E-F, Hollander, like Hollander. Well, he was a student of chemistry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spelled?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, H-O-L-L-A-N-D-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it that he told you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm, this was in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what period?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, it was about-- just before that, that October, well, this, this, this-- one of this [GERMAN] were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other forms of resistance that you were aware of about that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or throughout the period?

speaker: interviewee
text: --of this kind, of armed resistance. But I was all the time aware of a different form of resistance.

speaker: interviewer
text: That being?

speaker: interviewee
text: That being that in the resistance, not to give in, to stay straight, straight. Not straight, but you know, really straight-- decent, to be helpful to others. Not to-- well, my mother was always so furious when once I was furious, and I used dirty words. And my mother said, never again. If you are doing this, you are giving victory to the-- to Hitler. Because Hitler wants to make us the scum of the world. So don't do this. And I was-- she was right. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you, are  you clear?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to ask something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the general attitude in the ghetto toward resistance, either form?

speaker: interviewee
text: People never talk about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if as resistance, also you consider not to be let to be killed by the German, to run away, to run away. So they said everybody should run away. But everybody wanted to be among this one who run away. Now, there is a different thing. When one person run away, there was that collective responsibility. And it was punished by death. You see, if, well from one commando, it was said-- let's say, Liban. It was a quarry, people worked. One person run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do  spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Liban, L-I-B-A-N. As a matter of fact, there, for some times, was working the Pope John, the Pope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was working there too, once, in-- during the war. When from this Liban, one person ran away, the whole commando-- I don't remember how many people were there-- they were, they were executed. And you see, the collective responsibility was bounding people's hands. Who could-- I later on, when I was in another camp, I had all my papers. And I could just-- it was easy like this, to go out. Because that person who was on the-- well, like, like-- sentry, well, you know, at the door, at the door, he was our neighbor from before the war. And he always told me, you just go and never mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people who served as the guards at the gates and the sentries--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were different.

speaker: interviewer
text: --composed of what type of people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were different, in different-- in different camps, they were different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's talk about-- as far as the ghetto was concerned.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, you see he said that I could go. And I could not, because I knew that there is this collective responsibility. I will be free, and they will be dead?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember when that collective execution took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- well, this I don't remember. Because I was not in Liban. At this time, I was somewhere else. But I heard this,

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the evidence of that? You heard it through how?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I later on came back to Plaszow, well, there were the people who were colleagues, friends, mothers of the people who were executed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's backtrack, just for a moment, to the ghetto. Who served as the guards at the-- at the ghetto gates?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the ghetto gates, they were German, they were Polish policemen outside. And inside there were Jewish police.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Jews selected? What Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all day, Jewish policemen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you, they had uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had no guns, but they had stick. They had very special uniforms. And then this is how they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in terms of your being able to get in and out of the ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I needed a special permission. And the special permission could be given by the Jewish policemen, police, and with stamped by the German office at the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the general hours of entry and exit in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, usually it was-- depends for how long, but before 9  o'clock. At 9 o'clock, it was curfew. Everybody in the evening had to be home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. In terms of your living conditions, how sanitary was that room you lived  in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it depends how sanitary you were. Because, well, when you clean no matter what, then you clean. There were some places, which already when we moved in, people moved in, were terribly dirty, infested by, by insects and everything. Because this was the poorest part of Krakow. But, well, I remember when we moved into this one room, there were so many holes in the walls, which were full of, of, well, how do you call-- bedbugs, bedbugs. And then we-- it took us all day with my mother to clean them out and to paste them and to do so that they won't be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about privacy in one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Privacy? There was such a small, like, you know, [POLISH]. How do you say it? Like a-- like a, like a drapery on the-- well, thing. And naturally, one after another was going there. My young cousin, who was 14 at this time, the son of the engineer uncle, he was told, I was told. And I always have to yell, don't look! Disappear! Because, well, this was one room, and we were there so many. And we were just washing ourselves, one after another. So you see, this was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you think back during this period whether or not, amidst all of this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] were there moments of happiness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the moments of happiness, it was when we were all together. There were also moments of happiness, for example, when my brother came back from the POW camp. This was wonderful. When, after every terrible, well, transportation, or action, or selection, we found ourselves together and with friends and with [? tears ?] and with [? sad. ?] Well, this was that moment of, of happiness. Actually, well, I don't know. One, I was very happy one day when, when our dog came, just came through everything, like Lassie, come home. Well, but I had to-- we had to take him back. This was also a kind of a happiness. And of course, it was a different-- happiness is a very wide word. It's different. Happiness was every nice day. Happiness, it  was we were having a flower. Well, we were having two flowers on the window growing. So this was two. But first of all, it was more unhappiness than happiness.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about Jewish life, during this period in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were holidays. And in that orphanage, which, as a matter of fact, it was also in the building where before the war was Catholic Orphanage of Mercy-- of Catholic, well, [INAUDIBLE]. There was-- people were praying there. And it was-- people were praying so fervently. I don't remember that we were lighting candles on Shabbat. Because nobody had, well, candles also. But I remember how people were-- everybody was working. But nevertheless, people were telling one another, 'Happy New Year,' and maybe everything will change, this was.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's switch tapes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tom, we're still fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're still all right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In terms of social life, did you-- did you have a boyfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, having an older brother and house full of boys all the time, since I remember, my relation to boys and to men, generally, was somewhat different. They were-- I was used to fight with them or to, to-- you know. It was not such a big deal as with many other girls who were like this. And besides, I think I was too young or maybe too somewhat-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, it was enough that when someone was not using not a nice language or was not clean, had dirty fingernails, and this and that, it was, yuck. I don't want to have anything to do with them. And besides, many of the young girls and boys, they were drinking. And they were-- and I didn't like vodka. Well, I didn't like any alcohol.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somewhat, I was, I don't know. They had also a different relation to me somewhat. I was this one who is, well, writing songs and this, and it's good to talk and this and that. But in a way, they didn't dare to just, come on, and this and go into, and then let's, let's whatever, you know. It was not like this.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, my boyfriend, so to speak-- but it is difficult to say a boyfriend. It was a man, who was maybe 13, ah, heavens, 25 years older than I was. And what a man it was. I had no idea who this person was, actually, till three years ago, when I was in London, and I was checking on something for my-- another book.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the qualities at that time you respected so much in him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I--the same quality I respected in everybody, in everybody, but particularly in men-- that they are-- it's sensitive and understanding and strong. And so that one feels safe with him, and that is interesting in-- he has some-- not only in that money and in that making business. But also in, in-- that we can discuss books, and we can discuss literature and so forth. I can tell you a lot of him about in a moment. Because this, this was an unusual man, absolutely. But I did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] is there-- are there a couple of brief points you would want to bring up about him--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that you felt were

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to. Because-- well, it was at this time, when I was working in that electric store. And then, I, we had a bill for, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for, for some work there. And that boss told me, go, go, go and take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there. And well, I thought here, someone. And when I came there, this was-- he was living in the only one nice, modern home in the ghetto. And there he was, and he was a Viennese. And we spoke German. And he was an engineer, Alexander Weisberg. That was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was his name, Weisberg, W-E-I-S-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the main point is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Main point is that I felt that he-- the main point is that he, well, a few minutes later on, he came to this store. And we went for a walk. And then he walk me home. And he was a very elegant gentleman, and there was not too many elegant people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then he said, what would you like to do when-- in life? So I told him I would like to write books, and I would like to study. He said, something different. Every girl of your age wanted to be a film star. How come?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, so anyway. And then when he-- we met two or three times he was coming there. And then since when he said, I come into here. There was no way we can meet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told my mother, can I go to him? My mother said, of course you can. Well, he is a decent man, whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went there. He wanted to give me a kiss. And I said, I didn't come here for this. And then to make a long story short, he told me, if I am going to be your mentor, you listen. And he gave me the most beautiful poetry-- German poetry, Rilke, and so forth. And we had a very nice discussion and everything. And then he told me-- he was also this one who told me, you will survive, don't worry. But you will make your life very complicated.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the same guy, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same guy, Alexander Weisberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this-- this was taking place just before you left for--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was still in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, well, and then suddenly, we were supposed to go for some concert. I couldn't go. And I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother said, he was there with another. Well, I said, no, I don't want to know him anywhere. Bang. But-- OK? But two years, three years ago in London, someone who read my diary called me and said-- friend of my friends, Polish-- do you want to have photograph of Alexander Weisberg and his books? I said, yeah, why not? I came there, and there I found four books written by Alexander Weisberg-Cybulski.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is the guy who coined the term 'Iron Curtain.' He was a most important physicist, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] most important physicist who was director of-- in Russia, a communist. He was supposed to be the director of that great communist-- of that great, in Kharkov, place. And in 1936, he was, by Stalin, you know, sent to gulag.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, this is very important.
